cards.back: NOR gate. Y = NOT(A + B). Any ON driver pulls output LOW. Output HIGH only when all drivers are OFF.
NAND gate. Y = NOT(A · B). All drivers must be ON to pull output LOW. Any OFF driver keeps output HIGH.

cards.front: NOR truth table: A=0, B=0 → Y?
cards.back: Y = 1 (HIGH). Both drivers OFF → no path to ground → load pulls output HIGH. This is the only HIGH output case for NOR.

cards.front: NAND truth table: A=1, B=1 → Y?
cards.back: Y = 0 (LOW). Both series drivers ON → complete path to ground → output pulled LOW. This is the only LOW output case for NAND.

cards.front: Why is NOR preferred in NMOS logic?
cards.back: Parallel drivers: R_total = R_on/n (fast pull-down). Series drivers: R_total = n × R_on (slow, plus body effect). NOR switches faster.

cards.front: What is the body effect in series transistors?
cards.back: The source of upper transistors is above ground, increasing their effective VT. This makes them harder to turn ON and slows down NAND gates.

cards.front: Why are NAND/NOR called universal gates?
cards.back: Any Boolean function (AND, OR, NOT, XOR...) can be built using ONLY NAND or ONLY NOR gates. They are functionally complete.

cards.front: How many transistors in an n-input NMOS NOR?
cards.back: n + 1 total: n parallel drivers (one per input) + 1 load transistor.
NOR: output LOW in 3/4 states → more static power. NAND: output LOW in 1/4 states → less static power. NOR wastes more power on average.
Y = NOT(A + B) = A̅ + B̅. Also written as Y with a bar over (A+B).
Y = NOT(A · B) = A̅ · B̅. Also written as Y with a bar over (A·B).

cards.front: How does adding inputs affect each gate type?
cards.back: NOR: more parallel paths → faster but more static power. NAND: more series resistance → slower, worse body effect. NOR scales better in NMOS.
